title: Global Environment Facility (GEF)
focus: Institutional framework, genesis, and the five major environmental conventions funded by GEF.
rationale: GEF is a highly examinable multilateral fund. UPSC frequently asks about the specific conventions it funds.
sectionExcerpt: Global Environment Facility (GEF) is located in Washington, D.C., USA. It is the world's largest multilateral fund for the environment, established in 1992 during the Rio Earth Summit. The 1st Assembly took place in India in 1998. It acts as a financial mechanism for 5 major international environmental conventions: UNFCCC, UNCBD, UNCCD, Minamata Convention on Mercury, and Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs).
evidenceBullets: Established in 1992 during the Rio Earth Summit.
Acts as a financial mechanism for UNFCCC, UNCBD, UNCCD, Minamata Convention, and Stockholm Convention.
World's largest multilateral fund for environment, located in Washington, D.C.

title: World Meteorological Organisation (WMO)
focus: WMO's establishment, Global Atmosphere Watch Programme, and key findings from its Greenhouse Gas Bulletin and other reports.
rationale: WMO reports (like Greenhouse Gas Bulletin and State of the Global Climate) and its specialized agency status are prime targets for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: WMO is a specialized UN agency established in 1950, located in Geneva, Switzerland, with 193 Member States including India. Initiatives include the Global Atmosphere Watch (GAW) Programme. Key reports include the Greenhouse Gas Bulletin (noting atmospheric CO2 reached 423.9 ppm in 2024 and long-lived greenhouse gases increased radiative forcing by 54%), State of the Global Climate, State of Global Water Resources, and Air Quality and Climate Bulletin.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1950 as a specialized agency of the United Nations, headquartered in Geneva.
Coordinates global atmospheric monitoring networks through the Global Atmosphere Watch (GAW) Programme.
Publishes the Greenhouse Gas Bulletin, State of the Global Climate, and State of Global Water Resources.
Greenhouse Gas Bulletin 2024 reported CO2 levels at 423.9 ppm and a 54% increase in radiative forcing from long-lived greenhouse gases.

title: State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025
focus: World Bank's report on carbon pricing and the definitions of various carbon pricing instruments.
rationale: Carbon pricing mechanisms (ETS, Carbon Taxes, Crediting) are core economy-environment intersection topics for UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: The State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025 report is released by the World Bank Group. Carbon pricing puts a financial cost on greenhouse gas emissions. Instruments include Emissions Trading Systems (government sets a cap on emissions), Carbon Taxes (government levies a fee on emissions), and Carbon Crediting Mechanisms (tradable credits generated through voluntary emission reduction activities).
evidenceBullets: State and Trends of Carbon Pricing 2025 report is released by the World Bank Group.
Emissions Trading Systems involve the government setting a cap on GHG emissions or emission intensity.
Carbon Taxes involve the government levying a fee on covered entities for their GHG emissions.
Carbon Crediting Mechanisms generate tradable credits through voluntary activities that reduce emissions.
